text: Adhesions are visible in the upper midline and epigastrium, resulting from a previous large right subcostal incision that crossed the midline.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions, revealing the colon that is drawn up into these fibrous bands.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions in the upper midline and epigastrium, which are remnants from a previous open cholecystectomy.
The colon is visibly drawn up into the adhesions located in the upper midline and epigastrium, resulting from a previous large right subcostal incision.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions to allow the transverse colon to be reflected over the liver, facilitating the exposure of the mesentery for a medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon removes adhesions to allow the transverse colon to be reflected over the liver, exposing the mesentery for the medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon explores the space above the liver edge, searching for a tattoo marking on the colon but does not find it.
The surgeon breaks into the space above the liver edge, revealing the anatomical area for further dissection.
The surgeon completes the dissection of adhesions near the right subcostal incision, focusing on the area adjacent to the liver edge.
The surgeon encounters adhesions at the right subcostal incision site, manipulating the tissue to address these attachments.
The surgeon completes the dissection of adhesions at the liver edge, clearing the tissue from the surrounding area.
The surgeon reflects the omentum upwards, working to restore the anatomical orientation of the surgical field.
The surgeon reflects the omentum upwards, revealing the underlying structures in the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon continues to search for the tattoo while splaying the omentum upwards to visualize the iliocolic pedicle. The iliocolic pedicle is visible, and the surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon continues to splay the omentum upwards to visualize the iliocolic pedicle, while searching for the tattoo marking.
The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection, carefully separating tissue layers to progress the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, preparing to isolate the iliocolic vessels approximately one centimeter from their origin.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, manipulating the tissue to expose the iliocolic pedicle.
The surgeon isolates the iliocolic vessels approximately one centimeter from their takeoff, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon marks the anticipated path of the superior mesenteric vessels to ensure complete mesenteric removal, preparing to take the middle colic vessel due to a hepatic flexure lesion.
The surgeon marks the anticipated path of the superior mesenteric vessels on the mesentery to ensure adequate dissection during the procedure.
The surgeon plans to ligate the middle colic vessel due to the presence of a lesion at the hepatic flexure.
During the complete mesocolic excision, the surgeon follows the pathway closely, revealing the blue hue of the vein beneath the mesentery.
The surgeon isolates the iliocolic vein and proceeds to take it, followed by isolating the iliocolic artery. An anatomical variation is visible during this process.
The surgeon proceeds to isolate and take the iliocolic vein, carefully dissecting around the vessel to ensure clear visualization and control.
The surgeon isolates the artery, preparing to address the middle colic vessel due to the hepatic flexure lesion.
The surgeon identifies a vessel branching from the iliocolic, which is revealed to be the right colic artery, visible in the surgical field.
A vessel is visible branching off from the iliocolic vein, highlighted against the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon visually identifies the iliocolic vein, which is isolated and ready for further manipulation.
The surgeon identifies the right colic artery, highlighting its anatomical position and relation to surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the right colic vessel branching off from the ileocolic vessel, with a clear view of the bifurcation. A surgical window is created just above this bifurcation.
The right colic artery is visually identified branching off from the ileocolic artery.
The video shows a clear view of the right colic vessel branching off from the ileocolic artery.
The surgeon creates a surgical window just above the bifurcation of the right colic artery, visible in the operative field.
The surgeon approaches the right colic artery, opting to dissect it away from the superior mesenteric vessels before taking the right colic artery.

The surgeon identifies the tattoo marking on the retroperitoneal surface, indicating the absence of the right colic vessels. The focus shifts to the middle colic vessels branching from the superior mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon identifies the tattoo marking on the retroperitoneal area, indicating the absence of the right colic vessels. The next visible vessels to be encountered are the middle colic vessels branching from the superior mesenteric vessels.
The surgeon identifies and prepares to dissect the middle colic vessels, which are located off the superior mesenteric vessels.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection, carefully separating tissue layers to facilitate subsequent lateral dissection in the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection, carefully separating tissue layers in the abdominal cavity during the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon elevates the transverse colon to locate the root of the middle colic vessels, marking the mesentery at the planned distal transection margin, positioned just left of the middle colic vessels.
The surgeon elevates the transverse colon to locate the root of the middle colic vessels, revealing the anatomical structures for further dissection.
The surgeon marks the mesentery at the planned distal transection margin, located just to the left of the middle colic vessels.

The surgeon thins out the mesentery at the root of the middle colic vessels, encountering thicker tissue. Upon finding a suitable plane, the middle colic artery is identified and partially dissected, with further dissection needed to complete the process.
The surgeon thins out the mesentery at the root of the middle colic vessels, carefully manipulating the tissue to enhance visibility and access for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies a thicker tissue area and proceeds to dissect along a newly found plane in the mesentery near the middle colic vessels.
The surgeon identifies the middle colic artery during the dissection of the transverse colon, highlighting its location within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to thin out the mesentery at the root of the middle colic vessels, progressing towards the planned distal transection margin.
The surgeon identifies and takes a branch of the loop of Henle, followed by the visualization of the middle colic vein within the surgical field.
The surgeon excises a branch of the loop of Henle, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the middle colic vein, visually distinguishing it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon approaches the back of the stomach from a medial position, taking down the mesogastrium using an inferior approach.
The surgeon advances through the tissue plane, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon identifies the posterior aspect of the stomach, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon operates from a medial position, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon takes down the mesogastrium from an inferior approach, carefully separating it from surrounding structures.

The surgeon places a sponge to protect the duodenum and proceeds to dissect over the top, completing the distal margin mesentery. The proximity of the stomach is visible due to adhesions from a previous cholecystectomy.
The surgeon places a sponge to protect the duodenum and proceeds to maneuver over the top of the structure.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the distal margin mesentery, ensuring the area is fully separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon demonstrates the proximity of the stomach to the adhesions resulting from a previous cholecystectomy, highlighting the close anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the planned transection, revealing the stomach and attempting to access the previously dissected plane.
The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the planned transection, visually separating the tissue layers in preparation for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the stomach and attempts to enter the previously dissected plane, maneuvering carefully around the visible anatomical structures.

The surgeon identifies the area lateral to the previously dissected stomach, continuing to take down the mucogastrium and gastrohepatic omentum. The dissection progresses towards the gastroomental vessels.
The surgeon locates the area previously dissected from the stomach, moving slightly further lateral to continue the dissection.
The surgeon continues to dissect and remove the mucogastrium and gastrohepatic omentum, carefully separating these tissues from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon prepares to transect the gastropomental vessels, which are visible near the stomach and adhesions from a prior cholecystectomy.
The surgeon identifies a vessel and continues to take it down, having previously dissected the hepatic flexure at the start of the case, which is already visible as taken.
The surgeon identifies a vessel adjacent to the stomach, preparing to dissect within the previously established plane.
The surgeon continues to dissect and remove the remaining mucogastrium and gastrohepatic omentum from the stomach, progressing laterally.
The hepatic flexure appears partially dissected, as indicated by the visible separation of tissue, which was performed earlier in the procedure while searching for the tattoo.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection, addressing adhesions around the appendix resulting from a previous open appendectomy.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection, focusing on separating the tissue layers along the lateral aspect of the colon.
The surgeon encounters adhesions around the appendix, which are remnants from a previous open appendectomy performed years ago.
The surgeon attempts to free up the terminal ileum (TI) to achieve full lateral mobilization, aiming to reach the distal transverse colon for the planned anastomosis.
The surgeon attempts to free up the terminal ileum (TI) to achieve full lateral mobilization, manipulating the tissue to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon positions the mobilized transverse colon and distal transverse colon in preparation for anastomosis, ensuring alignment for the upcoming surgical connection.
The surgeon identifies sutures from a prior appendectomy and proceeds to complete the dissection of lateral attachments to fully mobilize the colon.
The video shows sutures from a previous appendectomy, visible around the appendix area.
The surgeon completes the dissection of lateral attachments to fully mobilize the colon, ensuring it is free from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon carefully dissects the terminal ileal attachments from the retroperitoneum, ensuring the ureter is not inadvertently included in the dissection. The ureter is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon addresses the terminal ileal attachments to the retroperitoneum, working to free these structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects tissue to ensure the ureter is not inadvertently included in the mobilization process.
The ureter is visible in the surgical field, clearly identified and separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the dissection, fully freeing the colon from its attachments. The small bowel mesentery is then taken, indicating the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the terminal ileal attachments to the retroperitoneum, ensuring the ureter is not involved in the process.
The surgeon has completed the dissection, and the colon is now visibly free from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon begins to take the small bowel mesentery, preparing to separate it from surrounding structures.

The surgeon checks the perfusion of the bowel and prepares to staple from the suprapubic port, which is upsized for specimen extraction. The small bowel is divided, minimizing incision size to reduce hernia risk.
The surgeon checks the perfusion of the colon and prepares to staple from the suprapubic port, which is upsized for specimen extraction, minimizing the risk of hernia at the incision site.
The surgeon divides the small bowel, completing the separation process.

The surgeon places the resected specimen above the liver and aligns the bowel segments for an isoparastaltic anastomosis. The anastomosis is performed using sutures, avoiding the use of staplers, while the two ends of the bowel remain stapled.

The surgeon temporarily places the specimen above the liver and aligns the bowel for an isoparastaltic anastomosis. The anastomosis is performed using a fully sewn technique, avoiding the use of staplers.
The surgeon aligns the bowel segments for an open anastomosis and begins sewing the outer back row of sutures, demonstrating the technique in real-time.
The surgeon prepares to perform a hand-sewn anastomosis, aligning the bowel ends for suturing without the use of staplers.
The surgeon aligns the bowel segments in preparation for an isoparastaltic anastomosis, ensuring proper orientation for the upcoming surgical connection.
The surgeon begins sewing the back row of the anastomosis on the bowel, using sutures to connect the tissue edges.
The surgeon is sewing the outer back row of the bowel anastomosis, aligning the tissue edges for secure closure.
The surgeon performs a colotomy and enterotomy, creating openings in the colon and small intestine respectively.
The surgeon performs a colotomy and enterotomy, making incisions into the colon and intestine.
The surgeon begins sewing the inner row of a two-layer colorectal anastomosis, extending the suture to the far corner to join the posterior staple lines and form the posterior outer row.
The surgeon begins suturing the inner row of the anastomosis, carefully placing stitches to secure the tissue layers together.
The surgeon begins sewing towards the far corner of the surgical site, using precise suturing techniques to secure the tissue.
The surgeon joins the posterior staple lines to form the posterior row of the outer layer in a two-layer colorectal anastomosis.
The surgeon begins the second inner suture in the opposite direction to ensure complete closure of the mucosal edges during the hand-sewn bowel anastomosis.
The surgeon begins the second inner suture of the colorectal anastomosis, sewing in the opposite direction to complete the inner layer.
The surgeon revisits the suture line to ensure complete closure of the mucosal edges, visually confirming the integrity of the anastomosis.
The surgeon checks the ICG fluorescence to assess tissue perfusion as they continue sewing to complete the anterior inner row of the anastomosis.
The surgeon checks the fluorescence of the tissue using indocyanine green (ICG) imaging as they maneuver around the corner of the bowel during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins to suture over the top to complete the anterior inner row of the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses the original suture to adjust the position of the knots, moving them away from the corner of the anastomosis site.
The surgeon uses the original suture to adjust the position of the knots, moving them away from the corner of the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the common channel in the colorectal anastomosis, finalizing the inner row of the bowel connection.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the common channel, finalizing the anastomosis in the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon joins two sutures and then uses the original suture to sew the anterior portion of the back row, completing the anastomosis.
The surgeon joins two sutures to secure the anastomosis, ensuring the connection between the tissue layers is maintained.
The surgeon uses the original suture to sew the back row anteriorly, completing the inner row of the anastomosis.
The surgeon places omentum over the surgical area, covering it as part of the procedure.
The surgeon closes the hernia primarily, ensuring the tissue is securely sutured to prevent complications during the healing phase.
